content.text: A CoE without a service catalog is a black box. Engineering teams don't know what to ask for, intake processes have no shared vocabulary, and the CoE's value is invisible to the executives who fund it. The service catalog solves this: it is the
menu of what the CoE offers
, who can consume each service, how to request it, and what SLA governs it. It also defines what the CoE
offer — equally important for boundary management.

attrs.alt: CoE Service Catalog: four tiers — Platform Services (CoE owns and operates), Standards & Patterns (CoE authors, BU adopts), Advisory & Review (CoE provides on request), Enablement (CoE delivers, BU attends). Each tier has 8 specific services listed with SLA targets.

content.text: Services split naturally into four tiers based on how they are consumed and who bears the operational burden:
Running infrastructure BUs call via API
Pull; teams read at their own pace

Tier 1 services have the highest operational cost and the lowest per-consumption cost — you build them once and they scale. Tier 3 advisory services have the inverse profile: low setup but high marginal cost per engagement. The catalog must be honest about this so BUs know when advisory capacity is scarce.
Platform services are always-on infrastructure. BUs consume them via API, SDK, or direct integration. The CoE is responsible for availability, security, and cost accounting.

The LLM gateway is the single egress point for all model API calls in the enterprise. It sits in front of every external model provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Azure OpenAI) and exposes a unified API to internal consumers:

# Internal consumers call the gateway, never the provider directly.
# Gateway handles auth, routing, rate limits, logging, and cost attribution.

import httpx

GATEWAY_URL = "https://llm-gateway.internal/v1"

async def chat_completion(
    messages: list[dict],
    model: str = "gpt-4o",         # logical name; gateway maps to provider/version
    bu_id: str = "engineering",    # for cost attribution
    max_tokens: int = 2048,
    temperature: float = 0.0,
) -> dict:
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {get_gateway_token()}",
        "X-BU-ID": bu_id,
        "X-Use-Case-ID": get_use_case_id(),  # from request context
    }
    payload = {
        "model": model,
        "messages": messages,
        "max_tokens": max_tokens,
        "temperature": temperature,
    }
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=60) as client:
        resp = await client.post(f"{GATEWAY_URL}/chat/completions",
                                 json=payload, headers=headers)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        return resp.json()

The gateway is where the CoE enforces model allowlists, per-BU rate limits, cost caps, and PII guardrails. A BU cannot call a disallowed model by routing around it — the gateway is the only egress path, enforced at the network layer.
The guardrail gateway is a separate service that intercepts both prompts (pre-LLM) and completions (post-LLM) and applies the enterprise safety policy:

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class GuardrailAction(Enum):
    ALLOW   = "allow"
    BLOCK   = "block"
    REDACT  = "redact"    # allow but strip matched spans
    WARN    = "warn"      # allow but flag for audit

@dataclass
class GuardrailResult:
    action: GuardrailAction
    reason: str | None
    modified_text: str | None  # set when action == REDACT
    policy_id: str

# Guardrail gateway SDK — wraps the internal REST service
class GuardrailClient:
    def __init__(self, endpoint: str, api_key: str):
        self._endpoint = endpoint
        self._api_key = api_key

    def check_prompt(self, text: str, data_classification: str) -> GuardrailResult:
        resp = httpx.post(
            f"{self._endpoint}/check/prompt",
            json={"text": text, "classification": data_classification},
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {self._api_key}"},
            timeout=5,
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        d = resp.json()
        return GuardrailResult(
            action=GuardrailAction(d["action"]),
            reason=d.get("reason"),
            modified_text=d.get("modified_text"),
            policy_id=d["policy_id"],
        )

    def check_completion(self, text: str) -> GuardrailResult:
        # Post-LLM: check for PII leakage, policy violations in output
        resp = httpx.post(
            f"{self._endpoint}/check/completion",
            json={"text": text},
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {self._api_key}"},
            timeout=5,
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        d = resp.json()
        return GuardrailResult(**d)

The model registry is the authoritative list of approved models with their metadata — capabilities, context window, cost per token, data residency, and approval status. It is the source of truth that the gateway uses to validate routing decisions:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class ModelEntry:
    model_id: str              # internal name: "gpt-4o", "claude-3-5-sonnet"
    provider: str              # "openai", "anthropic", "azure-openai"
    provider_model_id: str     # exact provider-side model string
    approved: bool
    approval_date: str | None
    context_window_tokens: int
    input_cost_per_1k: float   # USD
    output_cost_per_1k: float  # USD
    data_residency: list[str]  # ["us", "eu"] — affects GDPR/HIPAA routing
    max_data_classification: str  # "public" | "internal" | "confidential"
    capabilities: list[str]    # ["chat", "vision", "function_calling", "json_mode"]
    deprecated: bool = False
    deprecation_date: str | None = None
    notes: str = ""

Standards are authored by the CoE and consumed by BU engineers on their own schedule. They do not require a CoE engagement to use — they are self-service documentation. But the CoE is responsible for keeping them accurate and current.
A well-formed CoE pattern document answers five questions:
When to use this pattern (and when not to)
The canonical architecture (component diagram, not prose)
Cost and performance characteristics at scale
The worst pattern documents answer only question 3 — they are code dumps without context. The most-used ones answer question 1 first:
"Don't use this if your data is > 10k tokens and accuracy matters more than speed — use the parent-child chunking variant instead."
That sentence saves three architecture reviews.
All teams using enterprise data in prompts

Each document links to its source-of-truth repo (the CoE maintains a GitBook or Confluence space). Version history is public to all engineering teams — BUs can see when a document was last updated and whether their implementation is based on a stale version.

Advisory services are the CoE's highest-touch offering. They are time-bounded engagements where a CoE expert works directly with a BU team. Because they consume analyst time, they are gated by a request form and governed by a 5-business-day SLA for initial response.
CoE architect reviews BU system design for GenAI integration
Review memo: approved / conditional / reject with required changes
AI Risk Review Panel evaluates high-risk use cases
Risk memo with approval conditions and re-review date
CoE security specialist tests prompts for injection, leakage, jailbreak vectors
Findings report with severity ratings and remediation
CoE ethics lead + legal reviews proposed data use in prompts
CoE reviews BU eval dataset and metrics for a model selection or fine-tuning decision
Eval design feedback; benchmark pass/fail against CoE quality bar
CoE architect reviews tool definitions and permissions for agentic systems
Tool safety review; approval or required changes
CoE facilitates blameless post-mortem for GenAI production incidents
Post-mortem document; action items logged in risk register

Advisory requests flow through a lightweight intake form. The CoE program manager triages within 1 business day and assigns an owner with a committed completion date. BUs can track request status in the CoE's internal portal:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date, timedelta
from enum import Enum

class ReviewStatus(Enum):
    SUBMITTED    = "submitted"
    TRIAGED      = "triaged"       # owner assigned, SLA clock running
    IN_REVIEW    = "in_review"
    PENDING_INFO = "pending_info"  # CoE waiting on BU input
    COMPLETE     = "complete"
    CANCELLED    = "cancelled"

@dataclass
class AdvisoryRequest:
    request_id: str          # e.g. "AR-2024-089"
    service_type: str        # "architecture_review" | "risk_gate" | ...
    bu_name: str
    requestor: str
    submitted_date: date
    description: str
    priority: str            # "standard" | "expedited" (expedited: 2-day SLA, limited slots)
    status: ReviewStatus = ReviewStatus.SUBMITTED
    assigned_to: str | None = None
    due_date: date | None = None
    deliverable_url: str | None = None
    notes: str = ""

    def assign(self, owner: str, sla_days: int = 5) -> None:
        self.assigned_to = owner
        self.status = ReviewStatus.TRIAGED
        self.due_date = self.submitted_date + timedelta(days=sla_days)

    def is_overdue(self, today: date) -> bool:
        return (
            self.due_date is not None
            and today > self.due_date
            and self.status not in {ReviewStatus.COMPLETE, ReviewStatus.CANCELLED}
        )
Enablement services build the capability inside BUs so they need fewer Tier 3 advisory engagements over time. The CoE invests in enablement to reduce its own advisory load, not to create dependency.

Beyond formal training, the CoE runs an internal AI community — a Slack/Teams channel, bi-weekly office hours, and a monthly demo day. This is the cheapest and most effective enablement surface: engineers share what they've built, the CoE surfaces new platform capabilities, and guild leads socialize upcoming policy changes before they are enforced.
Measure community health by three signals:
(a lagging indicator of psychological safety with the CoE's authority function).
Publish SLAs for every service. Unpublished SLAs become oral commitments that vary by who the BU knows in the CoE — this creates inequity and erodes trust:
CoE Lead notified; P1 incident declared
Program manager escalates to CoE Lead
CoE Lead notifies Exec Steering; BU auto-approved with conditions
Updated within 30 days of triggering event
At least one cohort per quarter per course
Enablement lead escalates to CoE Lead

Auto-approval on SLA breach: if the Tier 2 risk gate is not completed within 5 business days and the CoE has not escalated requesting an extension, the use case is automatically approved at the BU's stated risk tier with a logged caveat. This prevents the CoE from becoming a bottleneck by inaction.
The catalog itself is a versioned document, not a wiki page. Version it like software:
Maintain the catalog in a structured format (YAML or JSON) so it can be rendered into multiple views — a developer portal, a Confluence page, a Slack app command
Assign a catalog owner (the CoE program manager) who reviews additions and removals quarterly
Require a proposed service to pass a readiness checklist before being listed: SLA defined, owner named, intake process documented, at least one BU has consumed it
Mark deprecated services explicitly (not silently removed); give 90 days notice before removal
Track consumption metrics per service: number of requests, time-to-complete, BU distribution, BU satisfaction score

# coe-service-catalog.yaml — versioned source of truth
version: "2024-Q3"
last_updated: "2024-07-01"
owner: "Jane Smith <jane@company.com>"

services:
  - id: llm-gateway
    tier: 1
    name: "LLM Gateway"
    description: "Unified API for all enterprise LLM calls with auth, routing, and rate limits"
    endpoint: "https://llm-gateway.internal/v1"
    sla:
      availability: "99.5% monthly"
      latency: "adds < 20ms p99 overhead"
    owner: "MLOps Team"
    docs_url: "https://docs.internal/coe/llm-gateway"
    status: "active"

  - id: architecture-review
    tier: 3
    name: "Architecture Review"
    description: "CoE architect reviews GenAI integration design before implementation begins"
    intake_url: "https://coe-portal.internal/request/architecture-review"
    sla:
      initial_response: "1 business day"
      completion: "5 business days"
    owner: "Principal AI Architect"
    docs_url: "https://docs.internal/coe/architecture-review"
    status: "active"
    capacity: "max 4 concurrent reviews"

  - id: genai-foundations
    tier: 4
    name: "GenAI Foundations"
    description: "5-day course covering LLM fundamentals, prompting, RAG, and responsible AI"
    schedule_url: "https://lms.internal/courses/genai-foundations"
    sla:
      delivery: "at least 1 cohort per quarter"
    owner: "Enablement Lead"
    status: "active"
    certification: true
    required_for: ["guild-membership"]

The CoE service catalog is the operational contract between the CoE and every engineering team in the enterprise. Without it, the CoE is invisible; with it, BUs know exactly what to ask for, when to expect it, and who is accountable when it doesn't deliver.
Structure the catalog in four tiers: always-on platform services, self-service standards, request-based advisory, and schedule-based enablement
Publish SLAs for every service — including an auto-approval clause for the Tier 2 risk gate so the CoE cannot be a silent bottleneck
Version the catalog as YAML or JSON; render it into a developer portal and a Slack command so engineers can find services without opening a ticket
Track consumption metrics per service quarterly; retire services with zero consumption and invest in services with long queues
Build enablement to reduce advisory demand over time — the goal is BU self-sufficiency, not CoE dependency
